hinge: Bleeding that cannot be controlled indicates inflamed gingival tissue from an ill-fitting provisional crown. The correct approach is to adjust or remake the provisional to achieve proper contours and margins, then re-evaluate after 2 weeks when the tissue has healed and bleeding has resolved.

An axial wall height of only 2 mm violates the retention and resistance form principle, which typically requires at least 3-4 mm of axial wall height for adequate retention. 1.5 mm interocclusal clearance, 0.5 mm supragingival margin, and marginal integrity are relatively adequate.

Short anterior teeth with brown fluorosis require a lapped (incisal overlap) veneer preparation to extend the tooth length and mask the deep discoloration at the incisal edge, while maintaining adequate retention form. Window preparation is too conservative; feather edge lacks retention.

Immediately after extraction, an ovate pontic design on a temporary FPD can be shaped to fit into the extraction socket, guiding tissue healing and creating a natural emergence profile. Modified ridge lap and conical designs are for healed ridges.

A metal post with threaded crown that dislodged 2 weeks post-cementation with the post and crown intact can be re-cemented if the preparation and post are sound. Only remake if the post or tooth shows damage or if there is recurrent dislodgement indicating fundamental issues.

A shoulder margin for all-ceramic crowns requires 1-1.5 mm width to provide adequate bulk of ceramic material and support the restoration against fracture. 0.5-0.7 mm is a chamfer dimension; 1.6-2 mm would be excessive.

Zirconia frameworks show marginal discrepancies comparable to or slightly higher than metal frameworks, and this is considered a disadvantage. Zirconia itself has high fracture resistance but may exhibit chipping of the veneering porcelain at the interface when bonding is insufficient.

Beveling for an onlay preparation is done using a diamond rotary instrument (flame or round-end taper diamond) in a high-speed handpiece. Chisels and GMTs are for refining internal line angles in cast gold preparations, not for beveling.

PFM crowns that appear overcontoured and opaque are aesthetically unacceptable because the metal substructure shines through, blocking light transmission and creating an artificial, bulky appearance. Undercontour would be thin and delicate; translucency would be more lifelike.

Resin-bonded bridge (Maryland) retainer preparation requires approximately 0.5-0.7 mm of lingual reduction with supragingival margins and proximal grooves or rest seats for retention. The minimum occlusal clearance for a metal retainer wing is 0.7 mm.

For a pt missing 14-17 and 34-37, 44, 47 (multiple posterior missing with no stable occlusion), a facebow transfer records the maxillary arch in relation to the condylar axis, and occlusion should be recorded in centric relation (CR) since the patient lacks a repeatable MIP due to extensive tooth loss.

A patient with history of MI can undergo elective PFM treatment if stable. Epinephrine in retraction cord should be avoided as it can cause tachycardia and increase cardiac workload. Plain cord or non-epinephrine hemostatic agents should be used instead.

Eccentric interference in protrusive or lateral movements with normal centric requires adjustment according to the BULL rule: Buccal cusps of Upper and Lingual cusps of Lower. For anterior interference, the lingual inclines of maxillary incisors are the primary contact. The answer should be lingual of maxillary, not incisal edge.

The primary esthetic advantage of dental ceramics is translucency — the ability to transmit and scatter light similar to natural tooth structure, creating lifelike restorations. Opacity and tensile strength are disadvantages; transparency is not the same as translucency.

A PFM crown with defective distal margins, food accumulation, bleeding, and tenderness to percussion has a compromised abutment. The first step is to remove the crown to assess restorability of the tooth beneath before committing to endodontic, periodontal, or replacement treatment.

Using a putty index made from a diagnostic wax-up to fabricate temporary restorations directly in the mouth is an indirect-direct technique. The wax-up is made indirectly on the cast, and the provisional is fabricated directly in the mouth using the putty matrix.

A crown that is higher than the occlusal plane on #21 with localized gingival swelling, isolated pocket, and tenderness to percussion has occlusal trauma from supraocclusion. The first management is occlusal adjustment to eliminate the premature contact and allow tissue healing.

Edge-to-edge occlusion creates heavy incisal loading on anterior teeth. Full coverage crowns provide the necessary strength and protect the tooth from fracture under these functional demands. Composite and porcelain veneers are weaker and at higher risk of failure under edge-to-edge loading.

A lateral incisor fractured at the gingival level with a long root needs orthodontic extrusion to expose sound tooth structure for a ferrule, followed by osseous recontouring to reposition the gingival margin. Post, core, and crown alone without extrusion would lack adequate ferrule.

An upper molar crown that glides anterior-superiorly during closure indicates a premature contact on the mesial incline of the maxillary cusp, deflecting the mandible forward. The mesial inclination is the cause of the interference.

Same preparation parameters as earlier question: 2 mm axial wall height is insufficient for adequate retention, violating the resistance and retention form principle which requires at least 3-4 mm of parallel axial wall for crown retention.

A protrusive interference from crown #26 indicates the mesial incline of the upper crown is too steep or long, contacting the opposing tooth prematurely during protrusion. The mesial inclination is the primary cause of this interference.

For bonding porcelain veneers, enamel should be etched for 15 seconds with 37% phosphoric acid. Longer etching (30 seconds) is for dentin; 20 seconds is sometimes recommended but 15 seconds is the standard board answer for enamel etching of veneer preparations.

With extensive missing teeth (17-14, 34-37, 44-47, all third molars missing), the patient lacks a stable MIP. Occlusion must be recorded in CR using bite blocks on record bases and a facebow transfer to mount the maxillary cast in correct relationship to the condylar axis.

Bubbles appearing under a retainer when water and pressure are applied indicate an open margin/gap between the retainer and abutment — air is trapped in the space and displaced by water under occlusal pressure. Food impaction under the pontic would not produce bubbles at the retainer cervical area.

For a young patient with good oral hygiene and sound abutment teeth missing a single central incisor due to trauma, the gold standard is a single implant-supported crown. It preserves adjacent teeth, provides the best esthetics, and has the highest long-term success rate.

A rounded bur produces a chamfer finish line — a curved, concave margin that provides excellent marginal adaptation for cast restorations, especially metal-ceramic crowns. Shoulder has a 90-degree flat margin; shoulder with bevel adds an angled bevel.

A mesially tilted molar (#37) prepared as an abutment for a 3-unit bridge will have a shortened mesial axial wall because the tilt reduces the height of the mesial wall that can be prepared within the tooth structure without over-tapering or pulp exposure.

Mild facial staining and incipient interproximal caries require a facial preparation approach for a veneer to address the esthetic concern on the facial surface, combined with conservative restorative treatment of the interproximal caries.

For restoring centrals, the protrusive scheme should be considered to ensure the restorations do not interfere during protrusive movements. The restorations should allow smooth anterior guidance without heavy protrusive contacts that could overload or fracture them.
